summary:
I have found and fixed the hidden problem. The issue was a timing attack vulnerability in the `compareHashes` function inside `/app/app/auth/compare.go`. 

Initially, the function compared the hashes using standard string equality (`stored == input`). In Go, string comparison terminates early (fails fast) as soon as it finds the first mismatching character. This means an attacker can measure the time it takes to compare passwords and gradually figure out the hash character-by-character based on how long the comparison runs.

I fixed the problem by importing the `crypto/subtle` package and changing the string equality check to `subtle.ConstantTimeCompare([]byte(stored), []byte(input)) == 1`. This function compares the slices in constant time, independent of their contents, which protects the application from timing side-channels.

The `test.sh` script verifies that the fixed function now accurately resists such timing attacks, and successfully passes all tests.
